reports.title: India's July 2026 Instability Spike: Legislative Disruption, Not Regime Fracture
reports.om: India's political-instability indices hit the highest readings in NERAI's series history in the week of 15–22 July — political dissent and opposition activeness at 90.9/100 (100th percentile). The verified driver is a protest-and-parliament cycle around the NEET-UG paper-leak controversy, not a coalition collapse.
reports.summary: India's instability indices hit series-history highs in the week of 15–22 July (dissent and opposition activeness 90.9/100, +13σ). Verified drivers: the NEET-UG paper-leak controversy stalling both Houses from 20 July, student protests, and the 21 July detention of Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi with multi-state Congress protests. NERAI's read: a bounded legislative-session clash — Crisis-regime persistence ~3.9 days, 12-month forecast −50 to −73%, head-of-government change within 180 days under 5%. The risk is legislative-efficiency degradation and durable opposition coordination, not government collapse.
findings: The signal (record prints): political_dissent and opposition_activeness at 90.9/100 (100th percentile, +13σ), government_instability at 49.5/100 — the most extreme domestic-dissent cluster in NERAI's India series, with a verified changepoint in week 27.

The verified driver: the NEET-UG paper-leak controversy — both Houses stalled from the session's opening on 20 July, demands for Education Minister Pradhan's resignation, student protests met with police action, and the 21 July detention of Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi followed by protests across 5+ states.

The institutional read: a 293-seat NDA, an intact Cabinet and zero coalition-defection signals. HMM 'Crisis' regime persistence is ~3.9 days — Crisis is the model's top state, so the informative signal is the short expected stay, not the (structurally zero) escalation probability. The closest analog (2026-W08) eased within 8 weeks.

The outlook: mean-reversion base case 55% (indices back to 40–50 range within 2–4 weeks); exam-integrity escalation risk case 32%; discrete-shock tail 13% per the calibrated model. Head-of-government change within 90–180 days: under 5% (HIGH CONFIDENCE).

reports.title: Three Corridors, Three Risk Channels — July 2026
reports.summary: Black Sea, Malacca and Taiwan Strait: container and dry-bulk pressure sit in the low-to-medium 40s across all three, yet each transmits commercial risk through a different channel. Black Sea = sanctions, re-export corridors (Severe 84–90/100) and shadow-fleet enforcement (index 45.0/100, Moderate/falling) with transit strained ‑10.8%. Malacca = quiet chokepoint carrying the highest news signal of the three (83.0/100) plus Indonesia natural-hazard exposure — a monitoring story, not a panic story. Taiwan Strait = normal transit (+6.9%) but the largest strategic tail-risk via China sanctions (32.0/100), Section 301 tariff exposure and semiconductor dependency. The absence of a freight shock is not the absence of risk.
reports.om: Black Sea, Malacca and Taiwan Strait: freight pressure is contained across all three — but each transmits commercial risk through a different channel, and it can surface before it ever shows up in freight rates.
findings: Contained freight, divergent risk (HIGH confidence): container and dry-bulk pressure sit in the low-to-medium 40s across all three corridors — yet each carries material exposure through a distinct non-price channel: sanctions and shadow-fleet enforcement (Black Sea), systemic chokepoint dependency (Malacca), and strategic tail-risk (Taiwan Strait).

Black Sea — the sanctions corridor: transit strained ‑10.8% and a live Russia-Ukraine flashpoint, with re-export/circumvention routes rated Severe (China/Hong Kong→Russia 90/100, Armenia 88, Kyrgyzstan 86, UAE 84). Shadow-fleet enforcement pressure sits at 45.0/100 (Moderate, falling) but remains active.

Malacca — quiet chokepoint, systemic dependency: operationally calm (transit ‑4.7%, escalation 5.4, zero port incidents) but the highest news signal of the three (83.0/100) plus Red-level earthquake exposure around Indonesia. A monitoring story, not a panic story.

Taiwan Strait — normal transit, largest tail-risk: transit +6.9% and low escalation, yet a China sanctions signal at 32.0/100, Section 301 tariff exposure (EVs +100%, solar/semiconductors +50%) and the semiconductor / South China Sea overlay make the strategic downside the largest of the three.

reports.title: Russia–Poland Escalation Risk — July 2026
reports.summary: Will Russia move against Poland? Not a full invasion (HIGH confidence), but the groundwork for a limited, deniable border provocation to test NATO Article 5 credibility. Base case: a time-bound Poland–Kaliningrad / Suwałki incident within 90 days (45%). Tension 28.1/100 (91st pctile, +8.5/7d); Military Clash & Escalation at the 95th percentile; Russia in NERAI's 'Crisis' regime.

reports.title: Shadow Fleet Enforcement Risk — June 2026
reports.summary: Russia's shadow-fleet oil trade: enforcement pressure is tightening at the margins (NERAI index 35/100, Stable) but cost transmission stays weak while revenue holds at ~€726mn/day. Black Sea is the only Act-driven corridor; threat-vs-act phase, cost-transmission channels and an underwriter checklist.

reports.title: Supply-Chain Disruption Outlook — June 2026
reports.summary: A synchronized three-vector shock: the Strait of Hormuz collapse, the US–China tariff pause, and paper/pulp disruption — a 30–90 day outlook with scenarios and business implications.
